name: Untitled
prompt:
[HOOK — 0:00–0:45]

[Upbeat, direct-to-camera energy]

You've built your own income. You work for yourself. You're making good money — maybe even more than your friends who are sat in an office nine to five. You can earn £80,000 a year… and still be treated like you're financially unstable. The moment you walk into a bank and say "I'm self-employed"… everything changes.

Suddenly they want two years of accounts, tax calculations, proof of this, evidence of that — and half the time you still get rejected.

Here's the truth: getting a mortgage when you're self-employed is harder. But it is absolutely not impossible. And most people who get rejected? They just didn't know the rules of the game before they walked in.

So in this video I'm breaking down everything you need to know — the documents, the traps, the tips — so you can actually get that mortgage approved.

If you're new here, welcome — I'm from Double Ur Hustle, and we talk about the real financial stuff that self-employed people actually need to know. Hit subscribe so you don't miss what's coming next.

Let's get into it.

⸻

[SECTION 1 — WHO COUNTS AS SELF-EMPLOYED? — 0:45–2:00]

First things first — what does a lender actually mean when they say "self-employed"? Because it's not about how you describe yourself — it's about ownership.

In the UK, a lender will class you as self-employed if you own more than 20 to 25% of a business and your income comes from that business. So this covers sole traders, freelancers, contractors, limited company directors, and business partners.

If you're on PAYE and getting regular payslips, even if you've got a side hustle on top — lenders will probably still class you as employed. That's actually in your favour.

But once your main income comes from your own business? The rules change.

One quick note for anyone working through an umbrella company — you'll likely need six months of payslips to prove your income, but you probably won't be treated as self-employed. Which is actually the easier route.

⸻

[SECTION 2 — HOW LENDERS ASSESS YOUR INCOME — 2:00–4:30]

This is the big one. And this is where most self-employed people get caught off guard — because lenders don't all assess income the same way.

If you're a sole trader or in a partnership, lenders will usually look at your net profit — that's your income after business expenses — over the last two to three years, and take an average. If your income's been all over the place, some lenders will use your lowest year as the baseline.

They're looking for consistency, not just income.

If you're a limited company director, it gets more interesting. Some lenders only count your salary plus dividends — which is what most directors pay themselves. Others will count your salary plus the company's net profit after tax. That second option can unlock a much higher borrowing amount, especially if you keep money in the business rather than paying it all out.

I've seen someone earning £90k only be assessed at £38k — purely because the lender only counted salary and dividends.

If you're a contractor, some lenders use a simple formula — your day rate, multiplied by five days a week, multiplied by 46 weeks. That can actually work in your favour if your day rate is solid.

And how much can you actually borrow? Most lenders cap it at around four and a half times your annual income — that's a ceiling, not a promise — but some will stretch to five and a half times if you're a higher earner.

⸻

[SECTION 3 — THE TAX TRAP NOBODY WARNS YOU ABOUT — 4:30–6:00]

[Lean in, slightly more serious tone]

Right, I need to talk about something that catches so many self-employed people out.

If your accountant has been doing a great job of minimising your taxable income — completely normal — that same low income figure on paper is exactly what the lender is going to use to decide how much you can borrow.

You can't optimise for tax and borrowing at the same time without planning.

Pay less tax now? Great. But borrow less later? Not so great.

This is a real trade-off, and there's no one-size-fits-all answer. But here's the practical tip: if you think you want to apply for a mortgage in the next one to two years, have a three-way conversation — you, your accountant, and a mortgage broker — so everyone knows what you're working towards.

And be careful about loading up expenses right before you apply. On your tax return, expenses reduce profit. Reduced profit means reduced mortgage offer.

⸻

[SECTION 4 — HOW LONG DO YOU NEED TO HAVE BEEN TRADING? — 6:00–7:30]

Most lenders want to see two to three years of accounts. Two years is really the sweet spot — that's where most mainstream banks and building societies start taking you seriously.

Some lenders still ask for three years, but they're in the minority.

If you've only been trading for one year — it's not game over, but your options are narrower. Halifax, for example, will consider one year of accounts if you also provide three months of business bank statements and an income projection for year two. Some lenders will want a bigger deposit to offset the shorter track record.

The minimum is twelve months of trading. Under that, you're going to struggle.

And if you're thinking of switching from sole trader to limited company before you apply, hold that thought. Some lenders will treat you as a brand new business even if you've been trading for years. Timing matters.

⸻

[SECTION 5 — WHAT DOCUMENTS DO YOU NEED? — 7:30–9:30]

[Clear and organised — slightly slower pace]

This is where organised people win. Get these ready before you approach a lender.

Everyone needs the basics — proof of ID, proof of address, three to six months of bank statements, and evidence of your deposit.

If you're a sole trader, you'll also need your SA302 — that's your tax calculation from HMRC — plus the Tax Year Overview that shows your tax has been paid. Most lenders want two years of these.

If you're a limited company director, add: signed company accounts certified by a qualified accountant, and an accountant's certificate confirming your salary, dividends, turnover, and profit. And those accounts can't be more than 18 months old.

If you're a contractor, you'll need evidence of your day rate and contracts from the last twelve months, plus proof of upcoming contracts if you have them.

If you show up without these, you're signalling risk. A disorganised application is a slow one — and slow applications sometimes fall apart.

⸻

[SECTION 6 — HOW TO MAXIMISE YOUR CHANCES — 9:30–11:30]

Let's talk about what you can actually do to improve your shot at getting approved.

Check your credit score before you apply — not after. If there are errors, dispute them. Get on the electoral roll if you're not already.

Aim for a bigger deposit if you can. The minimum is around five to ten percent, but fifteen percent is where more lenders open up. Some will ask for twenty percent if your trading history is short. More deposit, more options, better rates.

Keep your business and personal finances completely separate. A dedicated business account shows you're running things properly.

Make sure your tax is paid and up to date.

Avoid taking on new credit right before you apply — car finance, new credit cards — and avoid large unexplained transfers in the months leading up to it.

And be really careful about rejected applications. Every hard credit search gets recorded. Multiple rejections in a short space of time will damage your credit score. This is why doing your homework — or using a broker — before you apply matters.

⸻

[SECTION 7 — SHOULD YOU USE A MORTGAGE BROKER? — 11:30–12:30]

If you're PAYE? Maybe not. If you're self-employed? Almost always.

Every lender has different criteria for self-employed borrowers. Nationwide needs a minimum of two years' trading. Halifax will consider one year with the right supporting documents. Some lenders are great for limited company directors. Others work better for contractors.

A whole-of-market mortgage broker knows this landscape. They'll know which lender is most likely to accept your specific situation before you apply — which protects your credit score.

If you walk into your own bank and their advisor only sells their products, you're limiting yourself. For self-employed people especially, a whole-of-market broker is significantly more valuable.

⸻

[OUTRO — 12:30–13:00]

So there you have it. Getting a mortgage when you're self-employed is more complicated — but it's completely doable if you understand how lenders think, get your documents in order, and don't walk in blind.

The biggest things to take away: start planning early, speak to your accountant before you apply, and seriously consider using a broker who understands self-employment.

If this video helped you, drop a like — it genuinely helps the channel. And if you're self-employed and trying to figure out your finances, subscribe to Double Ur Hustle. This is exactly what we're here for.

See you in the next one.

scenes.title: Hook / Bank counter
scenes.description: The narrator stands at the lender's counter while a tall stack of documents and a warning icon sit between them. The lender explains what proof self-employed applicants need; the narrator looks overwhelmed but engaged. Upbeat, direct-to-camera energy.

scenes.title: Who counts as self-employed?
scenes.description: Split composition: the narrator (self-employed viewer stand-in) stands beside an income-scale graphic while a PAYE employee appears for contrast with simpler payslip energy. The lender gestures toward ownership percentages. Educational, clear framing.

scenes.title: How lenders assess income
scenes.description: The narrator and lender walk through net profit, dividends, and contractor day-rate math at a desk. An income scale and application funnel float between them as the lender shows how different rules change the borrowing ceiling.

scenes.title: The tax trap
scenes.description: The accountant unrolls an SA302 beside a tax-versus-borrowing seesaw while the narrator listens. The tone turns slightly more serious: minimised taxable income on paper is what lenders use to size the loan.

scenes.title: Trading history timeline
scenes.description: The lender traces a trading timeline from twelve months to three years of accounts while the narrator reacts. A beat on how switching from sole trader to limited company can reset the clock with some lenders.

scenes.title: The document pile
scenes.description: The narrator and accountant spread SA302s, accounts, and a thick documents stack across a table. The accountant organises while the narrator realises disorganised paperwork signals risk to lenders.

scenes.title: Maximising your chances
scenes.description: The narrator studies a credit score meter and a green checkmark list: electoral roll, separated accounts, no last-minute credit. Calm, practical, forward-looking—doing homework before applications hit the file.

scenes.title: Whole-of-market broker
scenes.description: The broker sits with the narrator, laptop open, comparing lender criteria. A funnel toward a house icon suggests shopping the market before hard searches—protecting the credit score.

scenes.title: Outro
scenes.description: The narrator faces camera with a warm sign-off; a checkmark and house icon reinforce takeaway and subscribe. Short, confident close.
